revision.comment: 記事を起こしてみました。
revision.text:
'''内湯''' (うちゆ) とは、[[温泉]]や[[銭湯]]などの入浴施設において、屋内にある[[浴槽]]をさす。屋外にある浴槽は[[露天風呂]]という。
{{onsen-stub|うちゆ}}

'''内湯''' (うちゆ) とは、温泉地にある[[旅館]]において、その宿が保有する[[風呂]]のこと。[[外湯]]の対義語である。古くからの[[温泉街]]や[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]の多くは、[[温泉]]は外湯のみで、各旅館は寝泊りする場を提供するのみであった。[[源泉]]開発の技術向上や、配湯設備が整うにつれ、各旅館が自前で風呂を保有するようになり、宿の外に出ずとも入浴できると言うことで内湯と呼ばれるようになった。
今日では、[[日帰り入浴施設]]や[[銭湯]]などにおいて屋内にある浴槽も内湯と呼ぶ。この場合の対義語は、屋外にある風呂である[[露天風呂]]である。
{{onsen-stub|うちゆ}}
[[Category:温泉|うちゆ]]

[[ファイル:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|240px|内湯の例]] '''内湯''' (うちゆ) とは、温泉地にある[[旅館]]において、その宿が保有する[[風呂]]のこと。[[外湯]]の対義語である。

[[ファイル:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|240px|内湯の例]] '''内湯'''（うちゆ）とは、温泉地にある[[旅館]]において、その宿が保有する[[風呂]]のこと。[[外湯]]の対義語である。
== 概要 == 古くからの[[温泉街]]や[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]の多くは、[[温泉]]は外湯のみで、各旅館は寝泊りする場を提供するのみであった。[[明治時代]]、[[大分県]]の[[別府温泉]]では[[源泉]]開発に[[上総掘り]]の技術が応用された湯突きが盛んになり、各旅館が独自の源泉を持つようになる。このような源泉開発はしだいに全国各地に普及し、各旅館が自前で風呂を保有するようになり、宿の外に出ずとも入浴できると言うことで内湯と呼ばれるようになった。
今日では、[[日帰り入浴施設]]や[[銭湯]]などにおいて屋内にある浴槽も内湯と呼ぶ。この場合の対義語は、屋外にある風呂である[[露天風呂]]である。

revision.comment: 出典が全くないため資料を基に編集
revision.text:
[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|内湯の例]] '''内湯'''（うちゆ）とは、[[温泉|温泉資源]]利用の一形態<ref name="高柳" />。温泉地の旅館等の宿泊施設に直接温泉を引き各施設内に温泉浴場を設置した形態をいう<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>。温泉地内（湯元）に共同の浴場（共同湯）として設けられる[[外湯]]の[[対義語]]である<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />。
== 概要 == 外湯と内湯の組み合わせは温泉地により異なり、温泉地の中心部に外湯を設ける例、温泉地の各所に湧出する泉源に外湯を設ける例、外湯と内湯が混在する例、内湯利用のみとする例の4つの型に分けられる<ref name="高柳" />。
機械による汲み上げが未だ無かった近世の温泉地では、湧出量が少なければ外湯として利用するほかなかった<ref name="高柳" />。湧出量が豊富な温泉地では内湯として旅館内に温泉浴場を設け、そのような旅館は「内湯旅館」として他の旅館とは区別された<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />。ただし、温泉資源が豊富な温泉地でも内湯利用を厳しく制限した例もあり、例えば[[熱海温泉]]では内湯利用に株が設定されていた<ref name="高柳" />。
近世には温泉療養（湯治客）が温泉利用の中心だったが、[[明治時代]]以降になり観光行楽目的の利用客が増えたことで内湯が日本全国に普及した<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />。さらにその後、内湯に分湯された温泉は、大浴場、家族風呂、個室バスなどに分割して使用されるようになり温泉の細分利用が進んだ<ref name="益子" />。
なお、屋外にある[[露天風呂]]と区別する意味で「内湯」を使用する例もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[外湯]] {{温泉}} {{Onsen-stub}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]] '''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of {{ill|Onsen|ja|温泉|lt=Onsen resources|lt_ja=温泉資源}} usage<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.
== Overview == The combination of sotoyu and uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />
In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉}}, stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />
During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />
Sometimes “uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.

For example, at {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉}}, stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />
During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />
Sometimes “uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]] '''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of {{ill|Onsen|ja|温泉|lt=Onsen resources|lt_ja=温泉資源}} usage<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.
== Overview == The combination of sotoyu and uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />
In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at {{ill|Atami Onsen|ja|熱海温泉}}, stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />
During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />
Sometimes “uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related topics == * {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯}}
{{Onsen-stub}}

revision.text:
[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|内湯の例]] '''内湯'''（うちゆ）とは、[[温泉|温泉資源]]利用の一形態<ref name="高柳" />。温泉地の旅館等の宿泊施設に直接温泉を引き各施設内に温泉浴場を設置した形態をいう<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>。温泉地内（湯元）に共同の浴場（共同湯）として設けられる[[外湯]]の[[対義語]]である<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />。
== 概要 == 外湯と内湯の組み合わせは温泉地により異なり、温泉地の中心部に外湯を設ける例、温泉地の各所に湧出する泉源に外湯を設ける例、外湯と内湯が混在する例、内湯利用のみとする例の4つの型に分けられる<ref name="高柳" />。
機械による汲み上げが未だ無かった近世の温泉地では、湧出量が少なければ外湯として利用するほかなかった<ref name="高柳" />。湧出量が豊富な温泉地では内湯として旅館内に温泉浴場を設け、そのような旅館は「内湯旅館」として他の旅館とは区別された<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />。ただし、温泉資源が豊富な温泉地でも内湯利用を厳しく制限した例もあり、例えば[[熱海温泉]]では内湯利用に株が設定されていた<ref name="高柳" />。
近世には温泉療養（湯治客）が温泉利用の中心だったが、[[明治時代]]以降になり観光行楽目的の利用客が増えたことで内湯が日本全国に普及した<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />。さらにその後、内湯に分湯された温泉は、大浴場、家族風呂、個室バスなどに分割して使用されるようになり温泉の細分利用が進んだ<ref name="益子" />。
なお、屋外にある[[露天風呂]]と区別する意味で「内湯」を使用する例もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[外湯]] {{温泉}} {{Onsen-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:うちゆ}} [[Category:温泉]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[内湯]] to [[Uchiyu]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

== 概要 == 外湯と内湯の組み合わせは温泉地により異なり、温泉地の中心部に外湯を設ける例、温泉地の各所に湧出する泉源に外湯を設ける例、外湯と内湯が混在する例、内湯利用のみとする例の4つの型に分けられる<ref name="高柳" />。

なお、屋外にある[[露天風呂]]と区別する意味で「内湯」を使用する例もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>。

{{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}

refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]]
'''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.

== Overview ==
The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />

In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at [[:en:Atami Onsen|Atami Onsen]], stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />

During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

'''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.

== Overview ==
The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />

In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at [[:en:Atami Onsen|Atami Onsen]], stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />

During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

== Overview ==
The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />

In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at [[:en:Atami Onsen|Atami Onsen]], stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />

During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11394155]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}

refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]]
'''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.

== Overview ==
The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />

In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at [[:en:Atami Onsen|Atami Onsen]], stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />

During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11394155]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}

refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]]
'''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.

== Overview ==
The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />

In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at [[:en:Atami Onsen|Atami Onsen]], stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />

During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. '''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11394155]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}

refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]]
'''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.

== Overview ==
The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />

In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at [[:en:Atami Onsen|Atami Onsen]], stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />

During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11394155]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}
refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]] '''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.
== Overview == The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />
In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at [[:en:Atami Onsen|Atami Onsen]], stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />
During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />
Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11394155]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}
refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]] '''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.
== Overview == The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />
In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at {{ill|Atami Onsen|en|Atami Onsen|WD=Q11569090|lt=Atami Onsen}}, stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />
During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}} onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />
Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.
== Overview == The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />
In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at {{ill|Atami Onsen|en|Atami Onsen|WD=Q11569090|lt=Atami Onsen}}, stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />
During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}} onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />
Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内湯]]
refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Yumura onsen25nt3200.jpg|thumb|220px|An example of an internal bath]] '''Uchiyu''' (内湯) is one form of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen water|1=Onsen}} utilization<ref name="高柳" />. It refers to a style in which a hot spring is piped directly into lodging facilities (such as ryokan) in a hot spring area, and each facility installs a hot spring bathing area within its premises<ref name="高柳">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}</ref><ref name="益子">{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}</ref>. It is the counterpart to {{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}}, which is established as a communal bath (kyōdō-yu) in the hot spring source area (yumoto)<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" />.
== Overview == The combination of Sotoyu and Uchiyu varies by hot spring area and can be grouped into four patterns: (1) establishing a communal bath in the center of the hot spring town, (2) creating communal baths at spring sources scattered throughout the area, (3) mixing communal and in-house facilities, or (4) having only in-house baths.<ref name="高柳" />
In early modern times, when mechanical pumping did not exist, any hot spring area with a small outflow could only offer baths as Sotoyu.<ref name="高柳" /> Where a spring source was plentiful, a hot spring bath could be installed within the ryokan as an in-house bath, and such ryokan were distinguished from others as “Uchiyu ryokan.”<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> However, even in hot spring areas abundant in resources, in-house bathing was sometimes strictly limited. For example, at {{ill|Atami Onsen|en|Atami Onsen|WD=Q11569090|lt=Atami Onsen|1=Atami Onsen}}, stocks or certificates were issued for in-house use.<ref name="高柳" />
During the early modern era, therapeutic bathing (tōji) was the main usage of hot springs, but from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}} onward, as tourism and leisure travelers increased, Uchiyu spread throughout Japan.<ref name="高柳" /><ref name="益子" /> Subsequently, the hot spring water piped to Uchiyu began to be divided for use among large communal baths, family baths, private tubs, etc., so the segmented usage of hot springs progressed.<ref name="益子" />
Sometimes “Uchiyu” is also used to distinguish indoor baths from outdoor {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mhlw.go.jp/content/000704519.pdf |title=公衆浴場における衛生等管理要領等について|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=厚生労働省 |language=ja}}</ref>
